meta.intro: entries logged across creative coding, interaction design, and experimental web.

projects.title: Telegraphy
projects.description: A morse-code typing interface that turns typed phrases into morse code — part study in signal encoding, part love letter to early telecommunications.

videos.caption: Signal pulse visualization during live transmission.

images.caption: The transmission panel with morse output display.
Grain overlay applied to mimic vintage display hardware.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: I wanted to explore how information compresses into rhythm. Morse code felt like the perfect bridge between language and pattern — something humans can both read and feel.

notes.heading: Process
notes.body: Built with vanilla JavaScript and the Web Audio API. Each character maps to a frequency-duration pair. The visual layer uses canvas to draw pulse waves that sync with audio output.

notes.heading: Reflection
notes.body: This project reminded me that constraints breed creativity. Limiting the alphabet to dots and dashes forced me to think about timing, silence, and the space between signals.

projects.title: Self-Scroller
projects.description: An auto-scrolling personal narrative page that drifts through fragments of biography, photos, and ambient sound — scrolling as storytelling.

images.caption: Opening frame of the auto-scroll sequence.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: What if a portfolio didn't wait for you to scroll? Self-Scroller treats the page as a slow film reel — the viewer becomes a passenger rather than a navigator.

notes.heading: Process
notes.body: Used CSS scroll-driven animations and intersection observers to choreograph content reveals. Timing curves were tuned by hand until the drift felt meditative, not mechanical.

projects.title: RevRide
projects.description: A browser-based racing prototype with procedural track generation and synthwave visuals — built as a final project exploring real-time rendering on the web.

videos.caption: Gameplay loop — track generation and speed mechanics.

images.caption: Neon track environment with speed HUD overlay.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: RevRide started as a question: can a semester-long web project feel like an arcade cabinet? I leaned into neon palettes and low-poly geometry to keep performance smooth in-browser.

notes.heading: Technical Notes
notes.body: Procedural track segments are assembled from prefab curves. Collision detection runs on a simplified mesh to avoid frame drops on lower-end laptops.

projects.title: Igor
projects.description: An experimental character study rendered through layered shader passes — exploring identity as something that shifts under different light conditions.

images.caption: Primary portrait pass with chromatic aberration.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: Igor is named after the lab assistant trope — the figure behind the experiment. Here, the experiment is portraiture itself: how much can you distort a face before it stops feeling human?

notes.heading: Status
notes.body: Still iterating on the displacement maps. Planning to add audio-reactive distortion tied to ambient input.

projects.title: Go Forth Supreme PLC
projects.description: A retro-styled puzzle game built for a game jam — navigate industrial logic gates to restore power to a crumbling factory floor.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: Our jam theme was 'broken systems.' We interpreted that literally — programmable logic controllers as puzzle mechanics.

notes.heading: Collaboration
notes.body: Co-developed with Team PLC. I handled level design and UI; my teammate built the gate logic engine in 48 hours.

projects.title: Enzio Ware Archive
projects.description: A narrative archive game where players restore corrupted memory files from a defunct software company — part visual novel, part file-system simulator.

images.caption: Archive browser with corrupted file tree.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: Inspired by early 2000s shareware aesthetics. The UI is the story — every dialog box and error message reveals lore about the company that built it.

projects.title: Nitelife Gallery
projects.description: A nocturnal photo gallery organized by color temperature and noise grain — built to showcase low-light photography as its own visual language.

images.caption: Gallery index sorted by warm/cool color bands.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: Most photo galleries default to white backgrounds. Nitelife inverts that — the UI recedes so the images feel like they emit their own light.

notes.heading: Process
notes.body: Images are tagged with dominant hue values extracted via canvas sampling. The sort algorithm groups photos into temperature bands at runtime.

projects.title: Crossfire Prototype
projects.description: An interactive Figma prototype for a multiplayer strategy interface — exploring how players manage concurrent actions under time pressure.

images.caption: Primary action panel with concurrent move indicators.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: Designed during a project interview sprint. The core UX challenge was communicating overlapping player intents without overwhelming the screen.

projects.title: Cookbook Prototype
projects.description: A tactile recipe app prototype emphasizing hand gestures and step-by-step flow — designed for kitchen contexts where touch targets need to be generous.

images.caption: Step view with swipe-to-advance interaction pattern.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: Cooking with messy hands means tiny buttons fail. Every interactive element in this prototype is sized for accidental taps and wet fingers.

projects.title: Wave Function
projects.description: Generative visuals driven by ambient audio input — sine waves stack and interfere to produce ocean-like interference patterns in real time.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: Wave Function treats sound as a physical material. Microphone input modulates amplitude and phase across layered sine functions rendered on canvas.

notes.heading: Process
notes.body: Started as a weekend sketch, evolved into a meditative installation piece. The ocean palette emerged naturally from stacking blue-shifted harmonics.

projects.title: Depth Chart
projects.description: A sonar-inspired data visualization that maps portfolio metrics as underwater terrain — depth equals time, brightness equals activity.

images.caption: Bathymetric-style render of activity over time.

notes.heading: Concept
notes.body: I wanted a self-reflective viz that felt native to this portfolio's ocean theme. Depth Chart reads like a sonar scan of my own commit history.

notes.heading: Next Steps
notes.body: Planning to hook this up to live GitHub data and add ping animations when new activity surfaces.
